name: Erlking
description: The Erlking (German: Erlkönig) is a sinister figure present in Germanic folklore, often depicted as a malevolent spirit or demon preying on children. His most famous representation stems from Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's poem "Erlkönig," where he appears as a supernatural being who lures a child to his death. The Erlking's origins are somewhat ambiguous, with some theories linking him to older folk beliefs about elves or other forest spirits. He is commonly envisioned as a shadowy figure, sometimes appearing as a king or nobleman, other times as a more monstrous entity. He uses persuasive words and promises of pleasure to entice children away from their parents or guardians. His touch is often fatal, causing sickness and ultimately death. The Erlking embodies the dangers lurking in the natural world, particularly the forest, and serves as a cautionary tale about the vulnerability of children and the deceptive nature of evil. He is a symbol of the unknown terrors that can befall those who stray from the safety of home and family. The Erlking's allure lies in his ability to exploit innocence and trust, making him a particularly frightening and enduring figure in Germanic folklore. He represents the dark side of nature and the ever-present threat of the supernatural. The creature serves as a warning against succumbing to temptation and the importance of protecting the vulnerable.
subtitle: Deathly king of the forest.
appearance: The Erlking's appearance varies, but he is often depicted as a tall, shadowy figure, sometimes dressed as a king or nobleman, other times as a gnarled and monstrous creature with piercing eyes and sharp claws. He is often associated with the forest and may have features that blend with the natural environment, such as bark-like skin or branches for limbs.
habitat: Dark forests, especially those near bodies of water or paths leading away from civilization.
behavior: The Erlking is a predatory creature that seeks out vulnerable children, luring them away from safety with promises of pleasure and fantastical lands. He uses persuasion, illusion, and sometimes force to achieve his goals. He is relentless in his pursuit and will not give up easily.

keyMyths.title: Goethe's Erlkönig
keyMyths.description: A father rides through the night with his sick child, who claims to see the Erlking. The Erlking lures the child with promises, eventually killing him.

keyMyths.title: The Alluring Voice
keyMyths.description: Many tales describe the Erlking's enchanting voice that promises safety and wonders in the forest, drawing children away from the protection of their homes and families. Those who listen are rarely seen again.

keyMyths.title: The Touch of Death
keyMyths.description: The Erlking's touch is said to bring sickness and death. Children who are touched by him quickly fall ill and fade away, their life force stolen by the malevolent spirit.

sources.author: Jacob Grimm
